channel.title: Incident-Response on Keith Kee KW
channel.description: Recent content in Incident-Response on Keith Kee KW

item.title: The Incident Manager's OS: OODA × RACI × Incident Commander
item.description:
Speed alone isn’t enough. A fast team without clear roles is just organized noise — tasks duplicating, decisions stalling, everyone looking at everyone else while the incident escalates.

The OODA Loop sets the rhythm. You need two more layers to make it work: role clarity and decision ownership.

Think of this as an operating system for your incident response. Every OS manages three things — process scheduling (tempo), resource allocation (roles), and system calls (decisions) — so applications don’t have to reinvent the wheel. The Incident Manager’s OS does the same for crisis response. It is the shared foundation that handles the three things that break in every major incident:

item.title: Your Incident Response is Stuck on Observe
item.description:
How I stopped running faster and started cycling smarter

I used to think a good incident response was about speed. Faster alerts. Faster diagnosis. Faster rollbacks. More dashboards. More eyes on the screen.

I was wrong. Speed without shared Orientation isn’t speed — it’s organized chaos.

The framework that changed how I run incident response came from a Korean War fighter pilot.

The OODA Loop: More Than Four Steps in a Circle

John Boyd was a US Air Force colonel who noticed something strange. His F-86 Sabre pilots were consistently beating superior Soviet MiG-15s. The MiG was faster, could turn tighter, and climbed better. On paper, it should have won every engagement.
